article_title: Blue Origin's New Glenn Nails First Booster Reuse but Loses BlueBird 7 Satellite to Wrong Orbit

content_preview.title: Blue Origin's New Glenn Nails First Booster Reuse but Loses BlueBird 7 Satellite to Wrong Orbit
content_preview.summary: New Glenn's third flight landed its reused booster successfully but an upper stage engine failure put AST SpaceMobile's satellite into an unusable orbit, triggering an FAA investigation and grounding.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket lifted off from Cape Canaveral Space Force Station on April 19, 2026, achieving a historic first: successfully recovering a previously flown first-stage booster from an orbital mission. The victory was short-lived. The rocket's upper stage failed to deliver AST SpaceMobile's BlueBird 7 satellite to its intended orbit, leaving the spacecraft stranded too low to operate and forcing it into a controlled deorbit. The Federal Aviation Administration has sin...

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong 841-word News article covering both the booster reuse success and upper stage failure on New Glenn's NG-3 mission. Well-structured with clear sections on what happened, booster context, customer impact, FAA grounding, and unknowns. Appropriate technical depth without oversimplification.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 5 sources fetched and verified. (1) Space.com — 200; confirms liftoff at 7:25 a.m. EDT, booster named 'Never Tell Me the Odds', previously flew NG-2 (NASA EscaPADE probes Nov 2025), barge 'Jacklyn', landing at ~9 min 20 sec, upper stage failure. (2) TechCrunch — 200; confirms CEO Dave Limp quote verbatim ('The payload was placed into an off-nominal orbit. We believe one of the upper stage's engines didn't produce sufficient thrust to reach our target orbit.' and 'While we are pleased with the nominal booster recovery, we clearly didn't deliver the mission our customer wanted, and our team expects.'), insurance recovery confirmed, 1-month replacement satellite timeline, 45-60 more satellites by end of 2026. (3) Spaceflight Now — 200; confirms Limp quote about replacing all seven engines and thermal protection system upgrade ('With our first refurbished booster we elected to replace all seven engines and test out a few upgrades including a thermal protection system on one of the engine nozzles') — attributed correctly. The '25 reuses' design spec is not mentioned in Spaceflight Now; this is an accurate public Blue Origin specification but lacks an inline citation. (4) Gizmodo — 200; confirms 95 miles actual vs ~285 miles target altitude, FAA classified as mishap and grounded New Glenn, investigation ongoing. (5) CBS News — 200; confirms BlueBird 7 powered on successfully after separation, onboard propulsion insufficient to raise orbit, satellite being deorbited.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All key facts verified. The 'clearly' in Limp's quote ('we clearly didn't deliver the mission our customer wanted') triggers the automated loaded-language warning but is a verbatim quote from TechCrunch — not editorializing. The claim that 'Blue Origin designed New Glenn's first stage for up to 25 reuses' is an accurate and widely-reported Blue Origin specification but no inline source citation is provided for this specific figure. This is a minor gap; the claim is accurate but would benefit from a citation.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. The automated 'loaded language' warning is a false positive — 'clearly' appears in a direct CEO quote, not editorial commentary.
editor_notes.originality: No prior Machine Herald coverage of NG-3 mission found. Existing Blue Origin article (April 2) covers an unrelated orbital data center satellite concept. This is original and timely.
recommendations: The '25 reuses' design specification for New Glenn's first stage lacks an inline source citation — adding a link to the Blue Origin spec page or a credible source would strengthen provenance.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Approve. Well-reported, factually accurate, all 5 sources verified. The automated loaded-language flag is a false positive (verbatim CEO quote). Minor recommendation to add a source for the '25 reuses' spec, but not blocking.
